cue: How do you describe someone who can't hold on to money?

es.meaning: To be a spendthrift; to burn through money.
es.example: Es un manirroto: le pagan el viernes y el sábado no tiene nada.
es.exampleTranslation: He's a spendthrift — paid Friday, broke by Saturday.
es.regionNote: Also 'gastar a manos llenas'. Reviewer to confirm MX currency of manirroto vs plainer 'gastalón'.

it.meaning: To spend money easily; to be unable to save.
it.example: Ha le mani bucate: lo stipendio gli dura una settimana.
it.exampleTranslation: Money runs right through him — his salary lasts a week.

bridge.note: Both blame the hands, and both break them — but differently. Italian punches HOLES in them so the money falls through; Spanish snaps the hand itself (mani-rroto, hand-broken) so it cannot close. One is a leak, the other a grip that failed. Same diagnosis, two mechanisms, and neither language reaches for the wallet.

interference.es_to_it: 'Avere le mani rotte' is not the idiom — the hands are bucate, holed. And it is avere + le mani, never essere.
interference.it_to_es: 'Tener las manos agujereadas' is understood but is not the Spanish idiom; the fixed form makes an adjective of it: manirroto.

review.notes: Discovered via an Instagram card set Joe shared 2026-07-20 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). ES counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED. Leak-vs-grip is the payload.
